inline_review:
commit c1ea383e2ece9175ef5c9c35920c6b00365df106
Author: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>
udf: Fix race between file type conversion and writeback

This commit addresses a race condition between udf_setsize() and
udf_writepages() by open-coding the folio writeback preparation under the
folio lock for files with inline data.

Link: https://lore.kernel.org/all/f622c01.67ac.19cdbdd777d.Coremail.luckd0g@163.com

> diff --git a/fs/udf/inode.c b/fs/udf/inode.c
> index 7fae8002344a..a659cd7d7ec0 100644
> --- a/fs/udf/inode.c
> +++ b/fs/udf/inode.c

[ ... ]

> -static int udf_writepages(struct address_space *mapping,
> -			  struct writeback_control *wbc)
> +static int udf_writepages(struct address_space *mapping,
> +			  struct writeback_control *wbc)
>  {
>  	struct inode *inode = mapping->host;
>  	struct udf_inode_info *iinfo = UDF_I(inode);
>  
> +	if (iinfo->i_alloc_type == ICBTAG_FLAG_AD_IN_ICB) {
> +		struct folio *folio;
> +
> +		folio = filemap_lock_folio(mapping, 0);
> +		if (IS_ERR(folio))
> +			return 0;
> +		/*
> +		 * Recheck inode type under folio lock when we are protected
> +		 * against udf_expand_file_adinicb(). Bail to standard writeback
> +		 * path if file got expanded.
> +		 */
> +		if (iinfo->i_alloc_type != ICBTAG_FLAG_AD_IN_ICB) {
> +			folio_unlock(folio);
> +			goto mpage_writeback;
> +		}

Does this code leak the folio reference?

filemap_lock_folio() looks up the page cache entry, locks the folio, and
returns it with an elevated reference count. The exit paths here correctly
unlock the folio via folio_unlock(), but do not appear to call folio_put()
to drop the elevated reference.

This omission could cause a folio reference leak on every writeback attempt
for an inline data file, permanently pinning the folio in memory.

> +		if (folio_prepare_writeback(mapping, wbc, folio)) {
> +			folio_unlock(folio);
> +			return 0;
> +		}

Should this condition be negated as !folio_prepare_writeback()?

folio_prepare_writeback() returns true when a folio is genuinely dirty and
successfully prepared for I/O (which includes clearing its PG_dirty flag).
It returns false if the folio should be skipped, such as when it is already
clean or truncated.

Because the check aborts when true, if the folio actually needs writeback,
the function clears its dirty state but skips copying the data to the ICB
memory buffer via memcpy_from_file_folio(). This would result in the inline
data modifications being permanently and silently discarded.

>  		memcpy_from_file_folio(iinfo->i_data + iinfo->i_lenEAttr, folio,
>  				0, i_size_read(inode));
>  		folio_unlock(folio);

Are folio_start_writeback() and folio_end_writeback() needed here to
synchronize the page cache tags?

While folio_prepare_writeback() clears the PG_dirty flag, it deliberately
leaves the PAGECACHE_TAG_DIRTY tag in the mapping's xarray. The filesystem
writepages implementation is normally required to call
folio_start_writeback() to synchronize these tags and subsequently call
folio_end_writeback().

Because this open-coded logic bypasses those calls, the dirty tags are never
cleared from the xarray. This could cause background writeback threads and
sync() operations to repeatedly find the same dirty tags but operate on
folios lacking PG_dirty, leading to unnecessary CPU cycles or livelocks.

> +		mark_inode_dirty(inode);
> +		return 0;
>  	}
> +mpage_writeback:
>  	return mpage_writepages(mapping, wbc, udf_get_block_wb);
>  }
